sentences.base_language: What are you doing this weekend?
I don't have any plans yet.
sentences.grammarNote: 없어요 (eopseoyo) = 'there isn't / I don't have'. Its opposite is 있어요 (isseoyo) = 'there is / I have'. These two verbs handle both existence and possession in Korean — there's no separate 'have' verb.

sentences.base_language: Shall we watch a movie together?
sentences.grammarNote: 〜(으)ㄹ까요? is a suggestion ending meaning 'shall we…?' or 'should we…?'. 볼까요? (shall we watch?), 먹을까요? (shall we eat?). It's one of the most natural ways to propose doing something together in Korean.

sentences.base_language: Sounds good! When works for you?

sentences.grammarNote: 어때요? (eottaeyo?) = 'how about it? / what do you think?' It's one of the handiest question forms — attach it after any time, place, or idea to propose it: 이거 어때요? (how about this?), 내일 어때요? (how about tomorrow?).

sentences.base_language: Great. What time shall we meet?

sentences.grammarNote: 기대되다 (gidaedoeda) = 'to be looked forward to / to be anticipated'. It's a passive-feeling form: literally 'anticipation becomes'. Koreans use this to express excitement about upcoming events. 기대해요 is also common and slightly more active.
